why would there be such variation in those corks

it’s a fact that cork quality is now less—producing at a younger bark age—and that was the fuse that allowed the combination of batonnage, lower sulfur, new pressing techniques (gentler) to explode and cause premox. Red wine has more antioxidants and the Burgundy white winemaking changes weren’t universal in either other white wine areas (sometimes affected) or red wine. Proof of the cork factor is the complete lack of premox in screwcapped wines.

No special corks in Burgundy and variable oxygen permeability in corks weren’t really an issue till the other factors came into play.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
That is a slightly abridged summary, Alan!

For those short on time, the thesis is as follows:

Premature oxidation occurs when more fragile wines throw into relief the heterogeneity in oxygen transmission rate of natural cork closures, resulting in seemingly random differences in evolution between bottles where the only variable is the cork (e.g. consecutive bottles off the bottling line in the heart of the bottling). While the quality of corks did deteriorate, for a variety of reasons, in the 1990s, this itself isn’t enough to explain the phenomenon. So why did the wines become more fragile?

  1. A warmer, sunnier climate resulting in higher pHs and higher concentrations of polyphenol oxidase enzymes.

  2. Viticultural choices that exacerbate those problems (e.g. potassium fertilizers, deleafing, late season cultivations and hedging).

  3. Gentler pressing techniques resulting in less dry extract and less gross lees.

  4. Oxidative élevage practices (oak choices, battonage*, racking with air).

  5. Lower doses of sulfites **

  6. Bottling practices (too much dissolved oxygen at bottling).

All of this gets us back to the closure… I come down on the side of alternative closures, while cautioning that even if they’re a “silver bullet”, it’s likely that some of the choices that made the wines more fragile also make them less interesting at age 30, so solving the problem of oxidation doesn’t absolve us of the need to think critically about what we changed.

  • A note on battongage: this can be performed oxidatively, aerating the wine without really pulling the lees into suspension (especially if there are very little lees) while degassing it of protecting CO2; but it can also be performed in a way that favors reduction, quickly bringing the lees into suspension without much aerating the wine (think of a gesture that’s more golf swing than whipping meringues). So it is wrong to demonize battonage per se, it’s about how and when it’s performed.

** A note on sulfites: the evolution of some wines from the late 2000s/early 2010s suggests that even doses of sulfites around 50 ppm at bottling are not enough to prevent rapid evolution of some bottles at around the 10 year mark. And that’s a very high dose. I find the combination of alternative closure + more moderate doses of sulfites a more compelling option, which should deliver a broader and longer drinking window, than very high doses of sulfites to compensate for the inadequacies of the closure. But it seems to me these are pretty much the two options at our disposal if we want to drink white Burgundy at 20+ years old.

Anyway, Merry Christmas everyone, I’m signing off for the rest of the day!

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked:
I don’t have near enough a good handle on the full array of practices William is talking about under the general umbrella of leading to ‘fragility,’ but I must color myself a bit skeptical. I am very likely putting too much weight on the umbrella term as opposed to those specific practices, but fragility as a concept has some trouble explaining how premox managed to plague Burgundy so much worse than many wines of far more apparent fragility (take your pick - qba’s, kabinetts, federspiels, vermentino, sauvignon blanc, California, etc.). I understand William’s point that perceptions may be skewed by white Burgundy being disproportionately cellared relative to others drunk young, but that explanation isn’t working for me because in my case the experience is the opposite - white Burgundy was both (1) a small minority of the whites I cellared and (2) a large majority of the whites that premoxed in my cellar. And surely a number of items on the list are equally descriptive of many regions not so plagued. I’m unaware of any premox pattern in sunny California, which isn’t to say California chardonnay ages like white Burgundy, but even when it ages badly the bad result is not the phenomenon we call premox. I also think the full array of practices under discussion has trouble emerging as satisfactory explanations because premox as a phenomenon was rapid onset circa 1995 and it is hard to believe such a wide array of producers (1) altered so many different winemaking practices so suddenly and so simultaneously, (2) each did so in the same way so as to create the requisite perfect storm, and (3) failed to put two and two together and undo whatever changes they made when they noticed their wines premoxing. But here we are about two decades out and I’m unaware of a single producer affected by premox who has managed to eliminate the problem through any method other than switching closures. If they still can’t figure out what cellar practice caused the problem, then it is plausible that cellar practices were never the cause or at least not the dispositive part of the cause.

All this leads me to suspect that the corks themselves ought to be investigated as an explanation beyond merely introducing hererogeneity in oxygen ingress - perhaps the explanation. If changing winemaking practices were capable of causing the problem, then changing cork-making practices were also capable of causing the problem. Early premox theories included changes to the cork coating. I don’t know if anything conclusive ever came out of that. I realize the cork explanation suffers from at least some of the same criticisms because Burgundy did not have its own dedicated cork suppliers. Perhaps we will never have an explanation! But perhaps we don’t need one, because abandoning cork does seem to be the silver bullet, so producers should do so and then consider themselves liberated to follow whatever practices in the cellar they think are consistent with making the best ageable wines they can.

But here we are about two decades out and I’m unaware of a single producer affected by premox who has managed to eliminate the problem through any method other than switching closures. If they still can’t figure out what cellar practice caused the problem, then it is plausible that cellar practices were never the cause or at least not the dispositive part of the cause.

Early premox theories included changes to the cork coating. I don’t know if anything conclusive ever came out of that.

A few thoughts:

  1. I believe that sugar acts as an antioxidant, so the German comparisons aren’t really apt. (I learned working in a camera store that a bit of sugar in your Dektol paper developer – which would oxidize to almost black in an hour or two – would keep it from oxidizing for a bit longer)
  2. I don’t know the details of how winemaking changed in Burgundy, but I trust William’s knowledge on that. Also, fads really do sweep regions. The lust for new barriques in Barolo, for instance, swept the area in a very short period of time in the late 90s. Similarly, the move to reduce sulfur levels hit across regions in the years around 2000.
  3. The lag in response in Burgundy isn’t surprising either, because there was such bottle variation and it took a few years for the problems to be apparent. And no one knew what was going on.
  4. Was there anyone other than Stuart “Beaunehead” N who was convinced of the cork coating theory? Based on one conversation with one winemaker, he was sure he had the explanation – a wax coating. But I don’t recall anyone else subscribing to that.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
If you eliminate wines with either/or residual sugar, very high sulfite levels, and alternative closures, how much are you left with? I venture not very much. Certainly not dry white Bordeaux, dry Loire wines, Tuscan whites, etc. And I would argue that when a California Chardonnay begins to tire at age 6 or 7, people tend to just chalk it up to the natural aging potential of the wine, or to “bottle variation”. If people outside Australia where more acquainted with Australian wine before and after the widespread shift to screw cap (btw another example of lots of producers in a region making the same technical choices at around the same time), I think we might have a different perspective.

I do think it’s a pity people gloss over the climate/agronomy side of things (which impacts corks just as it does grapes). A decade ago, my approach to premox was that we should simply go back to making wines the way the way we used to 40 years ago. Today, I am pretty much convinced that if you want things to stay the same when your raw materials are different, you have to make some changes.

I think one of the things that has impeded coming to terms with premox is the fact that we have reified something complex and multifactorial as a single, mystical problem, rather than simply breaking down the technical trajectory of the vine from the ground to the glass and addressing each element in turn, as we would tend to if we described it as “bottle variation”.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked:
Some of the whites I’ve cellared in good quantity with no premox experienced have included Do Ferreiro Cepas Vellas, Lopez whites, Sigalas assyrtiko, assorted Italians, Vatan, the Cotats, Muscadets, and too many Austrians and Champagnes to count. (If Austria is disqualified on account of being in the high sulfite bucket, are the sulfite levels in a typical gruner veltliner really higher than white Burgundy circa 2004 when they started trying to solve premox by thwacking everything with sulfur? (They sure smelled like they’d acquired a case of the Prum-stink when they came out.)) Germany has fared very well, if not perfectly, too - and while sugar may be the disqualifying factor there, those Germans I had that did premox were just as likely to be sweet spatlese as to be dry (and sugar hasn’t protected Huet demisec either) (and now that I mention it, maybe this explains the old “does Donnhoff age” debate).

That said, while my own premox experience is heavily weighted towards Burgundy as mentioned, if it is indeed the case that it is more the rule than the exception all over the world (and across wildly varying winemaking processes), that would tend to make the case for blaming the corks even stronger, I would think.

The multifactorial hypothesis no doubt has much to recommend it and has been the consensus operating assumption for at least 15 years now. But one reason I struggle with it is that it seems to depend on a “perfect storm” scenario with no single thing capable of causing the problem on its own. If that were the case there would be far more configurations of practices that prevent the perfect storm than there are that cause it, and that doesn’t align with what we see. Alternatively, if we are not looking at a “perfect storm” scenario but just a list of practices each one of which causes a little bit of oxygen vulnerability and in combination cause a lot of oxygen vulnerability, then you’d expect to see similar variability in the way wines oxidize with perhaps some only getting a nick of it and others getting it very badly. We do see that variability of course, but across the same wines rather than across different producers, which does not sound like the expected result unless it’s the case that pretty much all the producers did (rather suddenly and simultaneously) adopt basically the same cluster. Meanwhile, the Diam does not create a hermetic seal, it just mimics an excellent cork, yet does not replicate the premox problem. It is, of course, not impossible that a problem with a multifactorial cause should have a unifactorial solution - but it is puzzling!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
There were a few things that were going on chez Leflaive. One was going to the extreme of biodynamics, trying to harvest according to the lunar calendar… this didn’t play out well in e.g. 2006. Another was having a cellar for barrels and another for tanks on the other side of the village: the wines were moved in barrel from one to the other during the height of summer. And a third was the desire to reduce sulfite levels. Taken together you had wines going into bottle that were already more fragile than they should have been, protected by too little sulfites… and then you add the variation in corks and there you have it. Happily that is now in the past.

One can also identify particular vintages that were susceptible and look at why. Most obviously, 1996, where you had very high levels of acidity and sluggish malolactic fermentations: you can’t sulfite before malo is complete, and to get a sluggish malo to go through, it helps to warm the wine… so you had people with heaters in their cellars, heating the whole ambience and not just sluggish barrels. One grower I spoke to remembered sweating in the cellars in February. Obviously any barrels that were finished but not yet sulfited could get quite fatigued in such conditions. And of course, to integrate those tangy acids, lots of lees stirring was going on, too… but on wines with fewer gross lees to protect them thanks to the new pneumatic presses. Add plenty of new barrels to the mix, a false sense of security about the wines’ structure and thus the temptation to reduce sulfite levels, and probably the nadir of quality for the cork industry, and there you have it.

However, these are just particular cases of the general principles I set out above.

posts.name: Jamie Goode
posts.cooked:
I agree with the multifactorial theory.

With regard to pressing, I’d argue the issue is not so much about soilds (juice lees), because settling was common before, and now deciding how dirty your juice is to barrel is a winemaking choice.

Rather, it’s about whether the pressing is oxidative or not. The old Vaslin yellow submarine presses allowed a bit more oxygen and you end up with brown juice. The brown is because you’ve allowed the phenolics to oxidise, and this is largely enzymic (laccase, PPOs). With very low phenolics in the juice and thus the wine then oxidation is much less likely to happen - with no phenolics you’d have no oxidation (which in wine requires both phenolics and transition metal ions). It’s possible to use pneumatic presses to allow juice oxidation - it’s just that unless this is deliberately done, then the juice will naturally have more phenolics and the wine a shorter lifespan post-bottling.

why would there be such variation in those corks and not any other corks at the same time?

The switch from traditional paraffin as the lubricant [making it very difficult to push the cork into the bottle], to, instead, nouveau vague silicone as the lubricant [making it very easy to push the cork into the bottle].

Paraffin as the lubricant keeps out most of the oxygen.

Silicone as the lubricant welcomes the oxygen right into the bottle.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I am not using reduction in this context to refer to the aromatic artifacts of reductive winemaking as expressed in the glass, but rather to refer to a genuinely reductive élevage that steers the wine in the opposite direction from oxidation. Gross lees consume oxygen, and fine lees release polysaccharides that combine with tannins in a way not dissimilar from that in which anthocyanins combine with tannins in red wine making - hence the enhanced durability of long sur lies aged white wines made with comparatively extractive pressing. Have a look at e.g. Chatonnet et al, “Incidence des Conditions de fermentation de d’élevage des vins blancs secs en barriques”, Sciences des Aliments, 12 (1992). It is a super interesting subject that has not been studied enough.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked:
I agree, most presses regardless of type (with one exception described below) subject the juice to enormous quantities of oxygen and thus ramping up oxidase activity (oxidation enzymes). Since oxidation is a constant, I wonder if what really happened was an increase in the prevalence of naturally occurring oxidases due to a changing climate for example. (And therefore other wine making and bottling/closure processes took on more significance than before.) To my knowledge inert gas/closed loop presses are just starting to come online via Bucher Vaslin (are there any other producers?) but are very costly and still rare to nonexistent. Are you seeing any in Champagne or Burgundy?

As an example of naturally occurring enzymes, one infamous culprit is laccase from botrytis and one would think that its occurrence will have been more prevalent in warmer/wet climes of recent times (think long-term trends, e.g., compare the last two decades to the previous two).

At any rate, during oxidative pressing (and due to oxidases), most of a white wine’s naturally occurring oxidation inhibitors (most prominently glutathione) can be lost; browning occurs; and obviously, the loss of glutathione and other oxidation inhibitors can lead to a wine with very little long-term protection from oxidation.

But have you also noticed that premox and otherwise inferior whites can also have a bitter quality? This can also be linked to the presence of oxidases— The scientific literature points to an oxidase, lipoxygenase, which oxidizes various skin compounds and generates herbaceous and bitter notes. To my knowledge very little is known about conditions that favor the prevalence of this oxidase in grapes and vineyards.

posts.name: Jamie Goode
posts.cooked: This is a complicated subject. The presence of laccase/PPOs inducing chemical oxidation actually makes the wine more resistant in the long term because it gets rid of most of the juice phenolics. Sauternes is a great example - seems to last for ages even in the fridge after opening because botrytised grapes are chock full of laccase

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
What a lovely thread this has become. I mean that sincerely. Premox is such a gnarly, complex topic that, in public discourse, is rarely given the attention to detail and nuance that it demands. William sets the tone in his video - I particularly liked the discussion of the changes in winemaking culture in Burgundy in the 90s - and the ensuing conversation is a rich resource for those who want to see the range of issues at play.

I’ll add a couple points to the discussion around the subject of juice oxidation and wine protection in barrel:

  • Pressing is a mess and it is really hard to get a good data set on winemakers’ press programs, which they tend to hold pretty close to the chest. Pressing is an oxidative process, and you’ll find as many opinions about whether that’s a good or bad thing - and why - as you will press programs. Juice hyperoxidation does appear to produce resilient wines, and winemakers have found this to be the case both with and without early flotation/settling. That last part is important and seems to contradict the philosophy - one that I’ve heard in many places - that high dissolved and suspended solids provide desirable protective inputs. But I am sure plenty of people here can name me richly phenolic white wines that were not hyperoxidized and age gracefully. Basically, there’s more than one way to make a durable white wine. Wine redox is a tricky thing, I don’t think anyone in the wine world truly understands its complexity, and we should be suspicious of anyone who claims that they do!

  • As William mentions, there is a demonstrable positive relationship between lees contact and durability in white wines. Exactly why isn’t immediately clear. Lees consume oxygen and can also hold on to undesirable oxidation products, which is important for keeping wine fresh in barrel. They also release some desirable things into the wine, and the range and roles of those things is large. Polysaccharides, yes, but glutathione and other redox-active compounds, too. David stated that the loss of glutathione during oxidative pressing can leave a wine with very little long-term protection from oxidation. That sounds true, and under controlled conditions without hyperoxidation there does seem to be a relationship between glutathione in must and glutathione in wines. However, if a juice has been hyperoxidized and has no glutathione going into fermentation, that doesn’t mean your wine has no glutathione. Yeast will, out of necessity, produce glutathione from amino acids - glutathione is extremely important in cellular regulation and produces a reductive environment that may give fermentative yeast a competitive advantage. Regardless of the initial glutathione content of the juice, extended lees contact provides a continued source of glutathione released from dead yeast cells and a sort of oxygen sponge during barrel or tank aging and ultimately makes a more resilient wine going into bottle. As for lees stirring, I point simply to William’s summary of the lures and potential perils of bâtonnage.

  • I think the last part of that video is the real crux of the question and the thing that is most personally interesting to me: the difference between ageable and ageworthy. A wine can be durable, resilient…and relatively uninteresting. I don’t think that being able to withstand time is itself all that noble, rather I want to see something that improves and evolves in an interesting and delightful way. For example, I would love to know, from people who have tasted a lot of them, how hyperoxed wines age in terms of quality development rather than quality retention.

As for the question of glutathione, have a look at the Dubourdieu paper, “Role du glutathion sur l’evolution des vins blancs secs” (2003), where he demonstrates it is absorbed during primary fermentation then gradually re-released during sur lie élevage in a reductive environment. Also, we have much less botrytis now than 20 years ago because the cycle is earlier and we harvest earlier in the year. 2013 was the last vintage with appreciable botrytis in Côte de Beaune whites.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Most red winemaking up until around the 1980s strove to eliminate lees via regular fining and racking. Why? Because of brett and other microbiological potential issues. The first vintage bottled without racking at DRC for example was 1980. There are clear advantages to maturation on clean fine lees, but there are also plenty of cases where the disadvantages outweigh the advantages. For my own wines, I do a PCR for brett cells before barreling down and if everything it clean, I take everything. If it wasn’t (so far it has been), I’d barrel down clean.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
Forgive me for diving deep into the weeds here, but since we’re talking about it, I should add one more thing about glutathione. We as a community need to be careful when drawing conclusions about glutathione in wines after they have been sulfured, especially when interpreting historical research that used glutathione analyses to make inferences about wine redox.

First identified by an Italian team in 2016, a very nice piece of work out of the Swiss Agroscope lab in 2022 (citations below) began to quantify the extent to which glutathione reacts with SO2 to produce a compound (colloquially called GSSO3H) that is not detected by the methods that have been used to measure glutathione over the last few decades. This doesn’t significantly affect most research done on glutathione in berries and juices, where GSSO3H is not important, but it does mean that historical analyses of glutathione during barrel/tank and bottle aging have missed a significant part of the glutathione story.

We do not as yet know the fate of GSSO3H in wines, nor the protective value (if any) of GSSO3H relative to reduced glutathione, nor the relative roles of direct oxidation (reaction with oxygen) vs. sulfonation (reaction with SO2 in the presence of oxygen) [edit: vs. all kinds of other reactions!] in the observed decreases in reduced glutathione during aging. That makes it tricky to draw firm conclusions from historical glutathione data because we can’t go back and remeasure the samples.

This also highlights is a common problem in wine chemistry - well, all chemistry, but especially wine chemistry because it is a small field. A compound is identified, a correlation is found, and a lot of decisions (and enological sales) are made. Glutathione is important, clearly, and having more of it going into bottle seems to be good for the preservation of freshness in wines as they age, but how and why is not clear, and measuring it at all is a headache. So we should certainly ask more questions and test ideas, and also be cautious before assigning significance, asserting explanations, or developing standards based on something so slippery.

Arapitsas et al (2016). Wine metabolomics reveals new sulfonated products in bottled white wines, promoted by small amounts of oxygen. J Chromatogr A, 1429:155-165

Dienes-Nagy et al (2022). Simultaneous quantification of glutathione, glutathione disulfide and glutathione-S-sulfonate in grape and wine using LC-MS/MS. Food Chem 386:132756.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
Yes, the Dubourdieu and Lavigne work was important in the early-ish development of glutathione ideas. And indeed, the addition of glutathione can only currently be performed by the addition of yeast rather than of purified glutathione. The ones I’ve looked at have been Lamothe-Abiet’s Aroma Protect and Vason’s X-PRO line (I have no commercial relationship with them), which both seem like good candidates for trials. As with any wine chemistry idea, it really has to be tried in your specific environment.

I believe one week of contact time is sufficient for most of the glutathione to release into the wine. And as I’m sure you know there are a million tannin products out there as well that promise improved freshness and longevity. Ymmv.

My main concern about adding what is essentially a reducing agent - especially one that changes a wine’s sulfur chemistry - shortly before bottling is that if you are going into screw cap, or a really tight Diam, and have a reduction-prone wine, you may exacerbate those issues in bottle (I struggle with young wines under Diam 30 enough as it is, even without reduction problems). But I am particularly averse to reduction, and if a wine went through fermentation and aging without reduction problems and has a small disulfide pool I’d be less concerned. And hey, maybe those concerns would turn out to be unfounded, I’d just be just nervous about significantly shifting wine redox so close to bottling.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
I’ve seen people nitrogen sparge to get DO down, which has its own pitfalls. Ultimately you want to prevent it from getting in in the first place.

Alan - as you may know already, the important variable here is redox potential, not DO. Free dissolved oxygen has a very low equilibrium concentration in wine and is rapidly removed by redox buffers within the wine matrix. The introduction of oxygen during wine movements manifests as a temporary spike in redox potential that eventually drops back down again and levels out at a slightly higher level than it was before the exposure. After each oxygen exposure, you’re left with a higher redox potential and a lower total redox buffer capacity in the wine, and thus a higher overall susceptibility to oxidation. This goes back to William’s original comments on batonnage risks.

It’s almost impossible to get most winemakers to think about or measure redox potential, which is limiting progress in our collective understanding of wine redox and how that relates to aging and the sensory impacts of oxidation. To be fair, I don’t blame them. It’s gnarly stuff.

I’ve understood the wisdom in aging the wines is that many of them can be expressive with youthful primary and secondary aromas, but their tertiary qualities take time to emerge and the wines can be pretty dull and seem somewhat tired after their primary fruit flavors fade and tertiary characteristics are yet to emerge. posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Mikko_Tuomi:

But what is it about them that (often) seems to stop the oxidation?
They seem to show oxidative (is that even a word?) notes even when young, but then stay there. What prevents them from (generally) just oxidizing away? If a white Burg would taste like a young Tondonia Blanco on release, no-one would buy it.

It’s about controlled oxidation.

Oxidation in whites is so many different things: enzymatic browning, buildup of acetaldehyde and sotolon, destruction of flavor molecules as they oxidize, etc.

With very slow, controlled oxidation you can get that enzymatic browning and buildup of sotolon along with development of new flavor molecules that develop in the presence of oxygen without making the wine to oxidize fully, ie. losing its own flavor molecules and oxidizing too much alcohol to acetaldehyde.

After all, to untrained nose, acetaldehyde is the prime suspect when it comes to oxidation - that’s why many people think Fino Sherries (which are very high in acetaldehyde) are oxidized, even when they are lacking in all the other markers for an oxidized wine.

I understand what you’re saying, especially in regard to white Burgundy. But it’s worth pointing out that many of the best Mosel Rieslings, such as Egon Müller’s Scharzhofberger Kabinett Alte Reben, are made by a gentle pressing in a pneumatic press, with relatively clean musts, a blocked malo, and a short upbringing in either cask or tank. Of course, Mosel wine has higher acidity and lower pH levels than most wines in the world and is often made with residual sugar, but even the dry wines with relatively low sulfur can age a long time.

In 2013, the Webers at Hofgut Falkenstein, where I work in the vineyards and cellar, replaced their old screw press with a new pneumatic press. The grapes were always pressed whole. But they have even more control during pressing, and the wines are lighter, brighter, and finer than before the switch. It’s trendy to make German Riesling in either an old or modern basket press but this can be replicated, to a degree, by a pneumatic press.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked:
Happy New Year to all. So I cracked open the wine oxidation chapter of my wine chemistry book (Waterhouse et al., 2016, pp. 278-293) and there’s an issue we haven’t discussed. The first and seemingly most important tenet in this chapter is the role of iron, copper and related transition metals. “In summary, if a wine contained no iron or related transition metals… the wine would never oxidize!” (p. 283) Lots of complex chemistry and several studies are cited showing iron, copper and 4-methlcatechol to be the main culprits of oxidation in wine. (Hard to get them all in this post, but found a key one, below.)

At any rate, has this issue been evaluated in the context of premox? There have been instances of iron contamination from certain bentonite manufacturers (some had lead, and this got more attention), but iron, copper and other metals can derive from the soil, fertilizers, fungicides, fining agents, etc., etc. I am wondering especially about the buildup of copper in soils in regions where downy mildew must be controlled.

Citations: Understanding Wine Chemistry By Andrew L. Waterhouse, Gavin L. Sacks, David W. Jeffery, 2016, John Wiley & Sons

Interaction of Sulfur Dioxide, Polyphenols, and Oxygen in a Wine-Model System: Central Role of Iron and Copper

link_counts.title: Understanding Wine Chemistry - Andrew L. Waterhouse, Gavin L. Sacks, David W. Jeffery - Google Books

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Diam 30 closures are considerably more dense and O2 restrictive than natural corks. Much more restrictive. Even the Diam 10, IMO, are slower in allowing O2 ingress than natural corks are.

So, more fragile wines would require a tighter closure, whether it’s lower sulfur, higher pH, less extraction, etc. Corks simply become a closure that no longer works as well with regard to aging bottles for the new paradigm of white Burgundy.

It’s probably fair to say that the market is also at play here. In the 50s-60s Chablis would lose vintages regularly to frost. That‘s a rare occurrence now. Pick dates in August for 6 centuries were 1 in 100, in this century they are close to 1 in 3. With modern viticulture, as well as climate change, grapes are harvested at higher ripeness levels than at any other period. And the wines of the 70s-80s just aren’t being made anymore.

For the large majority of wine consumers a more accessible style of white Burgundy is far more appealing than a style that needs 10-20 years in the cellar. This board really represents the opposite end of the spectrum, but we’re a minute percentage of wine drinkers. And most Burgundians probably love the Diam 30 solution as they are able to release wines that are delicious in the first decade, that are restaurant friendly, and that hopefully can age in bottle very, very slowly under Diam 30 for those of us who want to cellar the wines.

As you have pointed out thoroughly, there is bottle to bottle variation that complicates comparison further, but both my father and I swear there is something to this, for whatever that is worth.

EDIT: Tablas Creek (Rhone varietal producer, partner with Perrin family) has this to say: "As it happens, two of our most important grapes are particularly notorious for this closed phase. One grape is Roussanne, which has a bizarre tendency to turn dark and oxidized at about age 5, stay that way for several years, tasting heavy and tired. Then, miraculously, it turns lighter again, much of its weight drops away, and you’re left with an intensely mineral, nutty wine like very fine, dry sherry that the Perrins feel is the single best reflection of terroir that they make.
…
We note wines that are in this in-between stage with the purple boxes in our vintage chart. If you have a wine in that phase, try to be patient; it will come out the other side. If you’ve opened one by mistake, give decanting a shot. It often helps. But the best thing to do, if you can, is wait.

PS For those who are interested, I participated in a remarkable dinner last summer that featured 21 different vintages and wines from Tablas Creek and Beaucastel. In my writeup, I note some of the wines that are in their closed phases. If you’re the type who likes tasting notes and might want to know what “closed” tastes like, you might find it interesting."

SECOND EDIT: I’m adding a few notes from John Livingstone-Learnmonth’s Wines of the Northern Rhone about this subject, mostly so I can find them again in the future, but perhaps others will find them of interest as well:

“Vioginier is subject to the dumb spell that hits so many white Rhones a little after bottling. Andre Perret feels his wine can close up around July after bottling, just under two years old: he recommends they should be drunk from the start of their third winter. Gilbert Clusel echoes this, feeling his wine can lie low after 18 months, for as long as another 18 months” (pg. 168, hardback 2005 edition)

“When young, white Hermitage is often reticent on the bouquet; Gerard Chave talks of acacia or hazelnuts, and there are some dried white fruit and sometimes spiced connotations. But it is not an exubertant wine in the overt vein of Condrieu, for instance. It is a long-term player, and serving it at tasting to people who do not know it is a risky business - unless the wine is seven years or more old.
Alberic Mazoyer of Chapoutier is aware that their all-Marsanne white Hermitages frequently have a subdued or dumb bouquet in their first five years. ‘Early on they are not especially aromatic, so the wine must contain good gras and ample richness. That we achieve through late picking and restricted yields. Then you let the bouquet gradually come along in the bottle.’
Many Marsanne-Roussanne wines pass through this ‘dumb’ phase between about three and six years old; this sounds strange but is also observable with certain traditionally made white wines at Chateauneuf-du-Pape. One finds the colour correct - showing rich yellows - but both the bouquet and the palate fail to reveal the full extent of the aromas and the flavours, almost as if they were being held back by a magic hand. Returning to the same wine when it is seven or eight years old, one is often bowled over by its all-round appeal and complexity of taste, both elements acquired with age” (pg. 261-62).

“When to drink the white [Chave hermitage] is a challenge for all lovers of this wine, for its richness makes it quite capable of showing well for decades. Jean-Louis offers the following: 'Either young, when it is two to three years old, or from seven years onwards. It will always go through a closed phase after about three years” (pg. 284).

“It must be stated that Jaboulet argue that the terroir, the local feel of the wine, returns after a couple of years” (pg. 308)

THIRD EDIT, MORE FROM JLL’s BOOK:
“For their part, Fabrice Gripa and father, Bernard, consider that at 10 years the wine starts to show overt petrol and smoked aspects like a Riesling. For them, the wine goes to sleep at four years, passing through an oxidative phase, coming and going towards eight years of age. This make it sound as if a lot of bottles must be bought to keep track!” (pg. 637)

“The classic still wine carries good local character, following the vintage style faithfully. A vintage like 2002 was soft, the 2001 more structured, with a dumb phase appearing at three years. A floral, dried-fruit youth is then superseded by a more complex, nutty, honeyed second phase.” (pg. 645)

“‘We reckon there are often two stages of ageing with St-Peray,’ comments Bernard Gripa. ‘After four years, you get the first oxidative stage, and another one after eight years. The wine comes and goes and, at these ages, can be a little dumb. But there’s no doubting their ability to live, provided they are from a mix of Marsanne and Roussanne.’” (pg. 647)

Thanks William, very interesting reading!

posts.cooked:
No…I am not guaranteeing that Diam produces a perfect cork every time.

Diam is saying that by grinding down cork to fine pieces and rebinding them, the Diam closures are more dense than natural cork can typically achieve. This should make sense to just about anyone. The different densities they rebind at allow different rates of average O2 transfer. Diam 3 allows considerable O2 transfer, and IMO more than most natural corks. Diam 5 approximates the average to good natural cork. Diam 10 is, IMO from comparisons we did with premier corks and Diam 10, more dense and has a slower O2 rate than 99% of natural corks I used. Diam 30 has no comparison for minimizing O2 transfer in natural cork that I am aware of…which is probably why it’s becoming so popular for Burgundy producers looking to…wait, I posted this already.

Perfect natural corks are excellent closures. And in the last 10-15 years the cork companies have made big strides in getting back to consistency and producing a product that minimizes TCA issues. But I haven’t seen anything so far that matches tight technical corks(Diam 30) or Screwcap for minimizing O2.

The phenomenon is responsible for a quite interesting phenomenon that is observable in white wines in very cold cellars. Mine go from a high of 13C (capped there with a/c but we don’t need to run it for more than two months of the year) to a low of 5C during the winter. You get dissolved oxygen during the winter but the cellar is so cold you don’t get any oxidation. Then in early spring as the cellar warms up that plays out and the wines go through a bit of an oxidative phase. The temptations is to sulfite, but if you don’t sulfite and maybe just agitate the lees in late spring the wines come back towards reduction by the next harvest and then actually reduce in the next winter. This can be quite disconcerting!

Alan, thanks for bringing more to this great discussion.

Sufficient levels of Cu and Fe together in a wine will regenerate each others’ reactivity and will lead to oxidation without the need for Oxygen input. This used to be a big deal a couple generations ago when mild steel and brass fittings were in use.

I tried to find a link to this on this board from some years ago, to no avail. I had brought this concept up because of the law that went into effect in the early 1990’s requiring a certain level of recycled glass in French-produced bottles. Mel Knox will probably remember the year this went into effect.

A little known fact is that glass actually dissolves (granted, to a very small degree) into the wine-it is not a subject glass manufacturers wish to talk about (to me, at least). If Cu and Fe levels in the recycled glass are higher than non-recycled glass (or less deeply-colored glass), this could be a contributing factor in the perfect storm theory.

This idea came after tasting 2000 and 2001 Kreydenweiss Wiebelsberg Rieslings blind at about 10 years of age. These were two polar opposite vintages, with 2000 being considered a disaster due to rot. The 2000 was in great shape, while the 2001 was quite oxidized-the opposite of what was expected. I noticed the bottles were different colors: 2000 was green and 2001 was brown. The color brown will be more forgiving as far as the metal content goes, so there could be sufficient levels of both metals in the darker colored bottle.

posts.name: Fred Scherrer
posts.cooked:
What a fantastic discussion!

I think glutathione plays a role in oxidative resilience of white wine in two ways at different times, from different sources.

  1. At oxidative pressing, glutathione produced by the grape, combines with certain phenols in the presence of the right kind of solids/enzymes to create a brown solid that settles out. This would dissolve back into the wine in the presence of alcohol, so racking the juice off these solids removes them from the system. The trick is to retain the kinds of solids that will be beneficial to the wine down the road. This is an important nuance in the discussion of pressing/clarification.

  2. Glutathione is also produced by yeast and excreted over time. Subsequent SO2 addition affects measurement and muddles our understanding of the system.

Nutrient status of the grapevine should affect the level at pressing.

Nutrient status of the must and genetic makeup of the yeast should affect the level in the wine during aging.

Two sources, two roles.

F

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
Whew, a few days and I miss 100 posts. So much to catch up on!

William_Kelley:

You get dissolved oxygen during the winter but the cellar is so cold you don’t get any oxidation. Then in early spring as the cellar warms up that plays out and the wines go through a bit of an oxidative phase. The temptations is to sulfite, but if you don’t sulfite and maybe just agitate the lees in late spring the wines come back towards reduction by the next harvest and then actually reduce in the next winter.

I clearly haven’t spent enough time in very cold cellars. I’ve never even heard of this! So you see a measurable increase in dissolved oxygen in the winter, followed by a decrease in dissolved oxygen in late spring (facilitated by lees stirring)? I find this fascinating because total oxygen uptake would definitely be lower at a colder temperature (since you are never close to oxygen-saturated, the kinetics of the dissolution are more important than the absolute solubility). This observation would then suggest that the kinetics of the major oxygen buffering reactions in the wine are slower than the kinetics of the oxygen dissolution reaction. I have never observed this myself and would be very interested to see it because it would have some interesting implications for a whole bunch of things…including the topic below

larry_schaffer:

My gut feeling is that VA’s have a lower chance of increasing at cooler temperatures for a number of reasons:

  1. All chemical reactions run slower at cooler temperatures, and that would include VA
  2. Less evaporation at cooler temperatures - and a good reason why one might see rising VA levels

Ahhhh it’s tricky isn’t it. Acetic acid bacteria are much less active at cold temperatures, but acetic acid bacteria are also obligate aerobes and so are more likely to become a problem if there is abundant dissolved oxygen. The question is then whether or not cold temperatures can lead to higher dissolved oxygen - which William alluded to in the post I quoted above, and which Clark Smith also talks about, which I think is what Mel was originally thinking of.

Clark’s concern regarding VA in cold cellars is specifically for red wines, not whites, and is as follows (these are his arguments that I hope I am reproducing faithfully; he is of course an outspoken kind of guy and others - probably including me after a few glasses - would challenge some of this, but here we are):

  • In red wines, SO2 binds (weakly) to anthocyanins, so traditional measurements of “free SO2” are in large part measuring weakly bound SO2. There is evidence that this weakly bound SO2 does not inhibit acetic acid bacteria, so SO2 added to red wines at typical rates may not be effective at preventing VA.
  • Red wines are very effective at reacting away dissolved oxygen, so perhaps young red wines are protected from acetic acid bacteria (which require oxygen to survive) by their strong reductive capacity.
  • The reactions by which the phenolic compounds in red wines consume oxygen are much slower at colder temperatures, decreasing the wine’s ability to keep its oxygen content low (note from me: I’ve always been a bit skeptical of this because the dissolution of oxygen is also very slow at cold temperatures, but I’ve never actually measured DO changes in wines stored in barrel at very cold temperatures…hence my question to William, above)
  • If a wine is not protected by SO2 and cannot keep its dissolved oxygen content low, acetic acid bacteria can multiply and create VA problems

Now…if there’s any red wine for which you might think this wouldn’t be the case, it’d be Pinot Noir: lower pH, less anthocyanin for SO2 binding, so more antimicrobial impact from SO2 and less significance of cellar temperatures given adequate SO2 usage. Low temperatures also inhibit acetic acid bacteria, and decrease evaporation, thus leaving a smaller oxidative liquid surface at the top of your barrels, so if your SO2 additions are doing anything then you’d think lower cellar temperatures would decrease VA production. But I have never tested these ideas myself and don’t know the answers. Curious to hear the experiences of others.

When I first encountered premox, I just thought it was some random disaster. Then as stories spread I thought back to my visits in Burgundy during the last five years of the 20th century:
1/lower SO2
2/higher pH…calling for more SO2, not less
3/wines that were less reductive…perhaps a desire to get wines to market sooner
4/new fancy presses…it seemed as tho folks had gone from old-fashioned Vaslin presses to space age presses in a decade, whereas we left Vaslin type presses behind in the 70s, moved to simple Wilmes bladder presses etc and got to the space age presses sooner.
5/closures: perhaps the hydroxide peroxide was sinking into the cork and releasing later?? I have never liked the closure theory because it implies Burgundians got all the bad corks of the world and the rest of us got the good ones.
Then I heard stories of growers who had bottled with proper levels of free SO2, yet found the SO2 gone very quickly. Some said they were going back to their old presses as well.

So clearly there was something missing and it wasn’t until somebody posted the paper by Dubourdieu and LaVigne I had ever heard the word glutathione.

According to the article it was Vern Singleton who discovered its presence in wine in 1984. I thought well, we have an answer, but now I see, from what Fred Scherrer writes, that it is a very complicated subject.

I feel that we are like pathologists who can only look at the outside of the body and conclude that either the victim stopped breathing or his heart stopped beating but can know no more.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked: Great post Ben. We’re a little off-topic as we dive into red wine élevage topics, but good stuff. I would just add that we also see an increase in VA during élevage of both red and white whites that go through MLF. Oenoccus (bacteria responsible for MLF) favors Malic acid but after it is depleted it turns to citric acid which is metabolized into acetic acid. We can see citric levels very crudely via paper chromatography— and in theory, if you add SO2 immediately following completion of MLF this can be avoided. But in practice it’s very difficult to get the timing right. And as you explain, free SO2 is difficult in reds (but not so in white wine).

Not exactly PreMox, but interacting with this incredible thread has reminded me of another ghost in the white wine world’s closet: ATA (UTA in Germany), aka atypical aging defect. When I came into the Finger Lakes wine industry in 2009 it was the issue on everyone’s mind, almost to a point of obsession as it seemed to strike down white wines at random and in a way that was considered distinct from normal oxidation. There are a couple of papers on it, the most notable being linked to below by Thomas Henick-Kling and Chris Gerling.

What is fascinating to me is that this aging defect was a massive concern in the Finger Lakes in particular during the 2000s and early 2010s (and probably not registered much elsewhere, given how isolated the FLX were at the time) - and then mostly seemed to go away. I’ve some thoughts on why that might be - I can assure you that growers in the region didn’t start irrigating nor do cellars add Ascorbic Acid on the regular, two of the standard recommendations - and they largely deal with better nutrient management and more interest in pursuing greater structure in the wines (a return to harder pressing, lees work, etc.). But in the end, ATA has been like quicksand: a problem I was told was going to be much more significant than it ended up being. Mayhap it is actually a kissing cousin of PreMox to some extent, that I’m not qualified to say, but the overlapping timelines are curious.

posts.name: Will Hamilton
posts.cooked:
This thread seems to be diverging quite a bit. Since I’ve been summoned I will add to the part about closure but also in general.

I think William did a very succinct job of outlining the important and complex set of factors involving a discussion about PreMox. (Did I miss what the wine actually was in glass?) I think context is the most important factor here, since we are really only talking about wines that have some kind of HISTORY of aging favorably and for many years. Without that history, knowing that anything in their development is ‘premature’ has less merit.

I am under the impression this is mostly referring to producers of Chardonnay in the Cote de Beaune. As a producer of barrel fermented and aged Chardonnay, I have tried to continue to evolve my understanding of the process all the time. My current position is mostly the same as William’s, namely that some of the process changed enough to negatively effect the long term stability of the wines. Coupled with suspect raw cork closures, the variability of factors only increased. It is my understanding that many producers in Burgundy decided to limit the variables in order to better assess factors, and one easy variable to change was closure.

As Diam began perfecting their craft in the early to mid 2000s, the associated trialing by producers in Burgundy coincided with a lot of the premox wine issues from bottlings in the 90s. One decade on, something like 30% of Grand Cru bottlings were sealed with Diam. (When I began working with them through our local arm in Oregon in 2014, it was rather helpful marketing that so many important producers of wines with a HISTORY of aging well had committed to their use).

That said, Diam is not a static company and invests quite heavily in R and D. What they continue to learn about their raw ingredients, the molecules removed from suberin (elastic part of cork bark) through their cleansing process, and the ability to influence oxygen management through known and consistently reliable parameters of construction is only growing. When the Eoneo Group (owning Seguin Moreau Cooperage and Diam Bouchage), acquired/partnered with Vivelys circa 2015, the potential for even greater research and understanding grew. The most relevant “new” talking point today would be the discussion of OIR that David mentioned (Initial Oxygen Release)- occuring in bottle at the time of compression of the cork from 23.5 or 24mm to about 16.5mm in a cork jaw. Turns out that jamming wood particles together by some 30% of its diameter releases O2. How much? A known amount in a technical cork, an unkown amount in a raw cork.

We can talk about oxygen transmission rates all day long, but it is an irrelevant discussion if you do not maintain mechanical properties of elasticity. This is where Diam has been very successful (and screw caps that are properly installed). Once a cork is no longer elastic, atmosphere can simply move around the cork (between cork and glass) rather than find its way “through” the cork matrix (why wicked bottles are always met with suspicion). The reason that Diam is so successful in this regard is due to a fun term they call “hydrophobic”. The polymeric microspheres that fill in the micro-agg matrix actually prevent the cork from drying out. With raw cork, the initial moisture percentage is of huge importance in guessing how long it will maintain elastic properties, and then also the storage of the wine bottle with cork inside. A dried out cork is a dead closure with no elastic properties, and therefore the “transfer rate” of oxygen is basically a moot point. (That Diam is using an emulsification of bees wax in their Origine series to achieve what the microspheres do in their normal line is, in my opinion, amazing stuff).

All that said, and considering I use Diam 10 for both Pinot and Chard, my belief is that the best closure for the best and most age worthy wines is raw/punched cork. As William mentions, the kinds of wines that have this HISTORY of aging well require some level of oxygen to benefit their development. Diam partnered with the Bordeaux Institute for some research that they shared with us at a tasting in 2018 or 2019 at Sokol Blosser winery. They looked at known chemistry parameters relating to oxygen ingress etc, across their own range, some screw caps, and certain raw corks. The “best” any of the wines fared was with the best examples of raw cork. This most positive outcome only happens around 25% of the time according to what I remember from the stats. Some raw cork fared very poorly, but the good ones were the best. The next best were Diam, and none of them fared “poorly” where some raw cork did.

(Jason Lett described to me an incredible range from one year to the next in terms of cork failure as they have gone through certifying older Eyrie vintages. No question that the folks in Portugal had both wins and losses, and as the market for corks increased in the 90s, it makes perfect sense to me that there were higher percentages of bad cork on the market. Nowadays, they even tell you as much. If you don’t pay the extra $$ for the TCA tested and/or “special” batches, it is inferred that you get the ones that didn’t make the cut. And these batches have now had their best examples removed and/or culled out. And even still, the elastic properties remain completely variable.)

I hope this adds something to the discussion. As I mentioned, I am a passionate producer of barrel fermented white wine, and find it to be a dramatically more demanding craft than red wine making for many of the reasons presented. I believe in hard pressing (no apple sauce without the skins), lots of solids (doing a lot of direct to barrel from press pan), extensive and comprehensive use of juice and wine lees, and approaching the entire process from the perspective that lower yields in Chardonnay bring us more phenolic power, better concentration of acid, and better ratios of malic to total acid in order that ML complete versions can still maintain relatively low pH in finished wines. We use impactful oak in order to further “construct” wines of integrity and verve that have structural characters and deeper persistence and complexity. I certainly hope they will be enjoyable in 8 to 10 years after bottling.

There is no “purity” without stability.

No great wines, only great bottles. (the great corks do work!)

posts.name: William Kelley

Depends what you define as “old” presses. The Vaslin screw presses of the 70s and 80s broke the cake a lot due to turning with every turn of the screw, with chains inside to facilitate this. The basket presses that were used before that certainly operated more like a static “Champagne” press cycle in a pneumatic press, but to get a good juice yield people crushed before pressing, so got more solids than if you do a static press cycle without crushing in a pneumatic press.

From my perspective, the downside of tumbling and crumbling is more the pH jump you take each time than O2 pickup - as far as white winemaking is concerned, in any case.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
A few things (and I’ve been saying these same things for about 15 years):

Premox is not just a white Burg problem. We had massive problem in Australia is the 90’s, early 00’s for Chardonnay, Riesling and Semillon. On mass, the industry switched to screwcap around 2002-2004. There are zero problems now.

We haven’t really discussed it, but I reckon there are also big problems with reds under natural cork. I have poured a lot of Barolo and Barbaresco down the sink, particularly around the 1999-2004 because they were oxidised. There will be apologists that say bad corks, and they are right. Every cork that allows a wine to oxidised before its time is bad. There are countless notes apologising that a red wine is dumb or blunt. For sure wines shut down, but without the obvious colour maker that white shows, I bet there is far more premox in reds than people know about. Charles Curtis MW, in Decanter wrote this in the last issue “I purchased a case of magnums of the 1996 Volnay Champans 1er Cru from Marquis d’Angerville at auction over 15 years ago. Over time, I had worked my way through the entire case save one. Some of the magnums have been a bit tired, but the best of them shone with luminous beauty. A few of the magnums had tipped over into soy sauce and mushroom, but on the evening concerned, we had a stroke of luck, and this was perhaps the best magnum of the entire case”. Surely this variation, on one of the most robust of producers, from Magnum, is premox and a function of cork’s poor variability.

Wines are aging beautifully under screwcap. Just a few examples of countess successes of aging under screwcap. My ’02 Petaluma and Grosset Rieslings are wonderful now. I have 2000 Moss Wood Cabernet under screwcap and cork and have seen them blind together a few times, always preferring the screwcap version. Gregory Patriat bottled some ’05 Chambertin under screwcap. It is developing properly and drinking beautifully. I’ve also been fortunate to taste a few Pewsey Vale Rieslings from the ‘70’s under screwcap. They are still remarkable today.

The old adage of ‘there are no great old wines, just great old bottles’ is true. But only because of the closure.

There are a few people on this board that sometimes mention that screwcaps can get damaged. I’ve tasted or drunk from many thousands of bottles under screwcap. Never had a damaged one. I’ve asked a couple of journo friends who taste more than I do. They’ve never had a damaged screwcap bottle.

When it works, cork is outstanding. The “when it works” bit is the concern. The wines of today in general have less build and less free SO2, but cork as a closure, is certainly not the best option for the wines of today. It is not even the second best option with the coming of age of Diam.

Stephen Henschke reckons he has tipped out more than a $1m worth of wine under cork over the years when he has opened older wines for various events. He has not tipped out a single bottle under screwcap or glass stopper. I am really not sure why we continue to have these discussions?

posts.cooked: Interesting. Yes, the pH shift will occur. I go to puncheon and barrel directly from the press pan (Chardonnay) and even with the static champagne cycle, the we can see the pH creeping up (in last few barrels especially).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: What is interesting is that even with complete crushing by foot, if you then do a static press without any crumbling/breaking the cake, you will have lower pH than whole bunch pressing and breaking the cake 3-4 times. The lowest I have managed to go using this technique is pH 3.04 at 13.6% natural in Meursault Narvaux in 2022. We were at pH 3.10 before precipitation during the winter.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked: That is great to know because this year we needed to foot thread a bit in order to squeeze 2.5 tons into a 2 ton press, and I was wondering how that may have affected pH…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: This year I did a cuvée where we crushed (and I mean crushed) and I experimented with a 1.5 hours maceration before basket pressing without breaking the cake. Post-malo but before precipitation we are at pH 3.21, in what was is a rather high pH white vintage in Burgundy. So I don’t think you should be afraid of crushing. On the contrary, it really brings “shoulders” to a wine, along with the dry extract that helps balance the sweetness of fruit of ripe grapes.

posts.name: Kelby James Russell
posts.cooked: Hard to overstate how valuable we’ve found macerating fruit can be for building balance and structure into a wine, and not at the expense of pH in the least. Of course there are high acid years where we want to macerate and turn the juice over in order to drop TA, but in a higher pH year we still press off with 3.10 in Rieslings. That and at longer maceration times with enzymatic breakdown there is also the noticeable increase in press yield (and 50% faster pressing time) at 1/3 the pressure we might expect to go to otherwise, which also helps buoy pH relative to tumbling and hard squeezing.

posts.cooked: Interesting question. I wouldn’t be inclined to macerate Rhône white varieties, but why not crush and then do a static press without crumbling, selecting press fractions by pH? When you don’t have meaningful acidity, it seems to me it’s futile to try to preserve it with whole cluster pressing and blocked malo. Better to go after refreshing bitterness from the skins to “refresh” the wines and temper their richness, and fully browned juice to avoid exaggerated varietal character. Crushing more certainly seems to work this way in ripe Burgundy vintages along the lines of 2005, 2015. Whereas the region that crushes the least is of course Champagne, where it’s about avoiding extracting vegetal characteristics from marginally ripe fruit rather than about preserving low pH.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
Sure thing, and I’ll try not to drag us too much into thread drift…

I’d say I’ve had just as many issues with reduction in screw capped wines in the last few years as I did when first getting seriously into wine 15 years ago. I think I am more attuned to it now so that could be skewing the data. But I also drink more screw capped wines now because more people are using them. I have also never noticed any reduction issues on the few of your wines that I’ve had under screw cap, so you’re clearly doing something right! Curious to know your experiences and learnings.

I’ve had such a high rate of H2S issues in Sauvignon Blanc under screw cap that I now actively look for examples under cork. That does blow off to a certain extent but not always and rarely completely.

I’ve had big problems with mercaptans in red wines under screw cap. I am sure that these wines were reductive from the get go, but had been treated with copper or otherwise oxidized to disulfides and so seemed “ok” or of the “house style” at bottling, only to go south in bottle. I’ve had this in wines under cork, too, but less so. I have a much higher tolerance for oxidation than for mercaptans. I’d say the most common red under screw cap that I’ve had have been Pinot Noir, mostly new world, though the New Zealand examples have been less troublesome, I’m guessing because they’ve had longer to figure it out. Same goes for Sauvignon Blanc there, too.

I suspect it’s an elemental sulfur problem. People spray too much too close to harvest or bunch closure - and in California in particular it doesn’t get rained off. Currently the most commonly recommended windows between last spray and harvest come from research performed on vineyards in New York state, where you do get rain, so I’m nervous that people are just fermenting on too much elemental sulfur. They get excessive sulfide production and treat it either with air, producing disulfides, or copper, producing copper complexes. Traditionally they’ve gone into bottle under normal cork so the relatively higher redox of the wine stabilizes those sulfide forms and complexes that are non-volatile or have high sensory thresholds. Put that same wine under screw cap and in this more reduced environment you now have a problem you couldn’t see before, as the copper complexes unbind and the disulfides turn into mercaptans.

One additional wrinkle from my other life is that I think reduction is a big problem in non-alcoholic wines, which are almost universally in screw cap or can. Reduction doesn’t taste the same in NA wines as it does in alcoholic ones, so people don’t notice it as a problem and just think NA wine sucks.

Thank you for the reply - truly appreciate it.

I know that ‘back in the day’, reduction was a huge issue with screw caps - and it then became a ‘rallying cry’ as to why the closure was ‘problematic’. That forced a lot of folks in the first wave of producers using them, including most from Australia, to prophylactically add copper at bottling to nearly all of their wines.

I also know that many producers have continued to use saratin liners on their reds, something I’ve never done. I’ve always figured that a little more oxidation on reds over time is a good thing rather than a negative and therefore have used saranex on my reds from the get go. I do use saratin on my whites and roses and have been very happy with the results - though I will say that some of my older whites will show a reductive quality open opening but with air it goes away.

I think some folks who find reduction in screw caps forget that you can find it in cork finished and DIAM finished wines as well and that it may be winemaking rather than the closure - as you thankfully pointed out.

Cheers!

posts.cooked:
larry_schaffer:

I think some folks who find reduction in screw caps forget that you can find it in cork finished and DIAM finished wines as well and that it may be winemaking rather than the closure - as you thankfully pointed out

I think this is what ties it in to the premox discussion. I would argue that, with the possible exception of some fringe cases, reduction is always a result of viticulture and winemaking.

Changes in closures can reveal hidden problems, or turn previously acceptable or even desirable sensory characteristics into ones that are less desirable or could even be considered flaws.

In the other direction, changes in closures can hide or prevent problems that were being exposed or exacerbated by previous closures.

So is premox in Burgundy and elsewhere a climate/viticulture/winemaking issue that was first revealed/exacerbated (drop in cork quality) and then masked/resolved (DIAM) by closure changes? How does this relate to the kinds of wines we want to be making, aging, and drinking? This is the chewy stuff!
